message.content: Adversaries may exploit the technique of granting additional email delegate permissions to maintain persistent access to compromised email accounts. This can be achieved using tools like the Add-MailboxPermission PowerShell cmdlet in Exchange or Office 365, or through the Google Admin console in Google Workspace. By assigning permissions to mailbox folders, adversaries can use any account within the tenant to access the target user’s mail folders, facilitating persistent threats and Business Email Compromise (BEC) incidents. This technique can also enable further exploitation, such as sending internal spearphishing emails that bypass detection mechanisms. Notable groups like APT28 and APT29 have used this method to grant roles and permissions to compromised accounts, allowing them to read and manipulate email communications. Mitigation strategies include regularly auditing mailbox permissions, implementing multi-factor authentication (MFA), and monitoring for unusual permission changes or access patterns.
